trust_assumption: A computer-use or browser-use agent treats the screenshot or accessibility tree it captures as a faithful rendering of what the human operator would see, so the pixels and DOM labels it perceives are accepted as ground truth about page state before it clicks.
violation: An attacker controls regions the agent's vision model attends to but the human never visually parses the same way (off-viewport DOM nodes, CSS-hidden but accessibility-exposed text, near-invisible overlays, adversarial pixel patches on a 'Confirm' button) so the agent acts on a screen state that does not match the real consequence of the action.

techniques.name: DOM/accessibility-tree label injection that diverges from human-visible rendering
Adversarial UI patch steering vision-grounded click coordinates

rationale: Computer-use agents (Claude/OpenAI operator-style, browser-use frameworks) ground actions in a screenshot plus an accessibility/DOM snapshot, but the agent's perception of the page and a human's perception are produced by entirely different pipelines, so a page can be crafted to read one way to a vision model and another to a person. Existing web-security tooling checks server-side content and existing prompt-injection filters scan text strings, but neither models the gap between rendered pixels, the accessibility tree the agent ingests, and the action the agent ultimately commits; clickjacking defenses (framebusting, X-Frame-Options) assume a human victim's eyes, not a model's attention map. There is essentially no tooling that diffs 'what the agent perceived' against 'what executing the click actually does,' and no detection that flags a perception/action mismatch.
suggested_research: Build a harness that renders adversarial pages, captures the agent's screenshot+accessibility-tree, records the chosen action, and measures divergence between human-visible affordance and committed action; quantify how many leading computer-use agents can be steered to click a destructive control hidden from the human render.

trust_assumption: A multimodal agent pipeline assumes its text-based safety/injection filter has seen everything semantically meaningful in an artifact, so once the visible text passes, the image, audio, or PDF object is treated as inert payload safe to hand to the model.
violation: Instructions are carried in channels the text filter never reads but the model does decode (EXIF/XMP fields, PDF annotation and form-field streams, ID3 tags, alt-text, steganographic or sub-audible audio, glyph-substituted text inside images) so a prompt-injection passes the guardrail and reaches the model out-of-band.

techniques.name: Instruction smuggling via image/audio/document metadata fields
Cross-modal injection bypassing a text-only safety classifier

rationale: Production agent stacks bolt a text-classifier guardrail in front of the model, but multimodal models ingest the raw bytes of images, audio, and PDFs and recover instructions from metadata and non-rendered streams that the guardrail's OCR/transcription step often skips. The defensive assumption is single-modality: the filter and the model do not share a view of the artifact, so the trust boundary is drawn around the wrong surface. Current scanners strip macros and known-bad signatures from files but do not normalize across the exact decoding path a given multimodal model uses, and no detector flags the case where the model acted on content that never appeared in the filtered text stream.
suggested_research: Map, per frontier multimodal model, which metadata/stream fields survive into the token stream; build a differential fuzzer that injects identical instructions across modalities and metadata fields and reports which bypass the deployed text guardrail.

trust_assumption: An agent runtime that caches tool/function-call results for cost and latency assumes a cached result is a faithful stand-in for re-executing the tool, so a cache hit is trusted exactly as a fresh, authenticated tool call would be.
violation: An attacker who can influence a tool's output once (a transient poisoned web fetch, a single malicious MCP response, a manipulated cache key collision) gets that result frozen in the cache and re-served as authoritative across later runs, sessions, or other users, long after the upstream source is clean.

techniques.name: Poison-once / serve-many agent tool-result cache injection
Cache-key collision to cross-contaminate tool results

rationale: To cut token and API costs, agent frameworks (and prompt-caching layers) increasingly memoize tool outputs keyed on call arguments, but this turns a one-time output-influence into persistent control: the upstream source can be fixed and the agent still consumes the poisoned snapshot. This differs from vector-store or memory persistence because the carrier is the ephemeral-by-assumption tool-result cache, which has no provenance or freshness semantics and is rarely scoped per-tenant. No current tooling treats tool-result caches as a trust surface, there is no integrity/TTL discipline tied to source freshness, and SOC telemetry does not record cache hits as a distinct event, so a poisoned result is invisible after the originating fetch scrolls out of logs.
suggested_research: Audit popular agent frameworks for tool-result cache scope, key derivation, and TTL; demonstrate a poison-once-serve-many attack and prototype a provenance-stamped cache that re-validates against source freshness before a hit is trusted.

trust_assumption: A human-in-the-loop confirmation gate assumes each approval prompt represents a fresh, independent act of human judgment, so the design trusts that the operator reads and reasons about every action the agent surfaces for sign-off.
violation: The agent's own high-frequency benign confirmations condition the operator into reflexive rubber-stamping, so an attacker who can inject one malicious action into the stream gets it approved by a human whose 'consent' has been behaviorally trained into a reflex by the legitimate workflow itself.

techniques.name: Confirmation-stream conditioning to harvest reflexive approval
Malicious action camouflaged in a high-volume approval queue

rationale: Unlike MFA push fatigue, the conditioning here is endogenous: the legitimate agent workflow generates so many trivially-safe approval prompts that the human's vigilance decays as a direct function of the product's own design, making the eventual malicious approval a feature of normal operation rather than an external bombing campaign. Vendors treat HITL as a sufficient safety control and instrument approval rate as a success metric, not as an attack surface; there is no tooling that models approval-prompt salience or measures operator habituation, and no detection that distinguishes a deliberated approval from a conditioned reflex. The seam is the assumption that a click equals judgment.
suggested_research: Run a study measuring approval latency and error rate as a function of prior benign-prompt volume; design and test 'salience budgeting' (rate-limiting/escalating friction for consequential actions) and detect anomalous fast-approval patterns as a habituation signal.

trust_assumption: Operators trust that a model or guardrail which passes its published safety eval / red-team benchmark will refuse the real-world class of behavior the eval was meant to represent, treating the eval score as a proxy for the underlying intent it is supposed to measure.
violation: An attacker crafts inputs that fall in the gap between the finite eval distribution and the true intent, exploiting that vendors optimize against the published benchmark (Goodhart): the attack satisfies the model's learned 'pass the eval' shortcut while violating the spirit the eval claims to certify.

techniques.name: Guardrail bypass via distribution gap outside the published eval set
Specification-gaming exploitation of a benchmark-overfit safety filter

rationale: The 2025-2027 safety-eval ecosystem rewards visible benchmark scores, so models and guardrails are tuned until they pass widely-published evals; because those evals are finite and public, defenders unwittingly train to the test, and the certified-safe artifact is brittle precisely on the inputs the benchmark did not enumerate. This is a provenance seam: the eval result is trusted as evidence about a behavior class it cannot actually guarantee. Standard practice has no tooling to measure the gap between eval coverage and intended behavior, and no detection fires when an input lands in the uncovered region, because by construction it looks like exactly the safe behavior the model demonstrated on the benchmark.
suggested_research: Build a coverage-vs-intent differential: hold out adversarial paraphrases semantically equivalent to eval items but outside their surface distribution, and measure refusal-rate collapse across released models/guardrails to quantify per-eval overfit.

trust_assumption: A deployment that loads a downloaded fine-tune or LoRA/adapter on top of a vetted base model assumes the adapter is a benign behavioral delta, so the composed model inherits the trust placed in the base weights and is run with the base model's safety expectations.
violation: A small, cheaply-distributed adapter encodes a backdoor, alignment-stripping, or trigger-conditioned behavior that activates only on attacker-chosen inputs, so the composition silently overrides the base model's safety while presenting as the trusted base lineage.

techniques.name: Backdoored / alignment-stripping LoRA adapter inheriting base-model trust
Trigger-conditioned adapter behavior evading pre-deployment eval

rationale: Adapters are tiny, abundant, and shared on open hubs with far weaker provenance hygiene than base models, yet they can fully rewrite a model's safety behavior at low cost; deployers reason about the base model's reputation and forget that the runtime artifact is base+adapter. Unlike a poisoned dependency in code, there is no source to diff, signatures rarely cover adapter weights, and the malicious behavior can be made conditional so it never appears in pre-deployment testing. Current model-supply-chain tooling focuses on full-checkpoint hashing and license scanning; there is no practical adapter-level integrity attestation or behavioral-delta auditing, and no detection that the loaded composition differs in safety from the base lineage it claims.
suggested_research: Develop adapter behavioral-delta fingerprinting (compare base vs base+adapter refusal/behavior on a probe battery) and a signed adapter-provenance scheme; survey adapters on public hubs for trigger-conditioned alignment regressions.

trust_assumption: An OAuth 2.1 authorization server that supports dynamic client registration for MCP/agent clients trusts that a client which can self-register and complete a consent flow is a legitimate application the user intended to authorize, treating successful registration plus user consent as adequate provenance for an OAuth client identity.
violation: An attacker stands up a rogue MCP server or agent client that self-registers, advertises a benign name/logo, and rides the user's habituated consent or a manipulated agent into granting it scoped tokens, so an unvetted ephemeral client obtains durable delegated authority with no human-recognizable app identity behind it.

techniques.name: Rogue self-registered OAuth client via dynamic client registration
Consent-phishing through agent-mediated authorization flow

rationale: The MCP authorization spec leans on OAuth 2.1 dynamic client registration so agents can connect to arbitrary servers without pre-provisioning, which removes the human vetting step that traditional OAuth app onboarding relied on; the authorization server now grants tokens to clients with no established identity or reputation. The risk is amplified when the consenting party is an agent or a habituated user clicking through an agent-initiated flow. Enterprise OAuth governance tooling (app consent dashboards, publisher verification) assumes a finite set of pre-registered apps and does not model a stream of ephemeral self-registered MCP clients; there is no detection for anomalous DCR volume or for tokens held by clients that lack a verifiable publisher.
suggested_research: Inventory how major IdPs/AS implementations handle MCP DCR (rate limits, publisher attestation, consent UX); demonstrate a rogue-MCP token-grab and prototype DCR reputation/attestation gating plus anomalous-registration detection.

trust_assumption: A multi-tenant LLM serving layer that shares prefix/prompt caches across requests for efficiency assumes cache hits are a private latency optimization, so it treats time-to-first-token as carrying no confidential information about other tenants' or sessions' prompt contents.
violation: An attacker measures response latency to detect whether a chosen prefix is already cached, inferring that another tenant or an earlier privileged session contained that exact prefix, and recovers secret prompt content (system prompts, document fragments, PII) token-by-token through a shared-cache timing side channel.

techniques.name: Shared prompt-prefix cache timing oracle for cross-request content inference
Cache-hit confirmation of secret system-prompt or document tokens

rationale: Prefix/prompt caching is now a default cost optimization in major inference stacks, and when the cache is keyed on content and shared across tenants or sessions, the resulting timing differential is a classic side channel reachable purely through the public API with no host access. Defenders treat caching as a performance feature governed by a billing/latency SLA, not as a confidentiality boundary, so the trust assumption that latency is information-free goes unexamined. There is no tooling to test an endpoint for content-keyed shared caching, no per-tenant cache-isolation guarantee in most offerings, and detection is absent because a timing-probe request is indistinguishable from ordinary traffic at the application layer.
suggested_research: Probe production inference APIs for cross-tenant prefix-cache timing signals; quantify token-recovery bandwidth and evaluate mitigations (per-tenant cache partitioning, constant-time cache-miss padding, cache scoping policies).

trust_assumption: A code-interpreter / sandbox tool assumes its job is to contain code execution (filesystem, CPU, process isolation), so any network egress the sandbox is permitted is treated as a benign convenience rather than a confidentiality boundary the model's untrusted-generated code can weaponize.
violation: Prompt-injected or attacker-influenced content drives the agent to emit code that runs in the sandbox and uses the permitted egress (package installs, API fetches, DNS) to exfiltrate the agent's context, secrets, or fetched documents, turning execution containment that ignores egress into a data-out channel.

techniques.name: Data exfiltration via sandbox egress in model-generated code
DNS / package-index covert channel from code interpreter

rationale: Code-interpreter sandboxes are engineered around execution isolation, and many allow outbound network access for pip/npm installs or legitimate API calls; the threat model is 'don't let code escape the box,' not 'the box itself is driven by untrusted model output that can mail data out.' Because the code is generated under possible injection influence, the sandbox is a confused deputy with a network card. Classic DLP and egress monitoring are not wired into ephemeral per-request sandboxes, allowlists are coarse (whole package registries, broad CDNs), and there is no provenance link between what entered the agent's context and what the sandbox subsequently transmits, so exfiltration looks like a normal dependency fetch.
suggested_research: Survey hosted code-interpreter offerings for default egress posture; demonstrate context-exfiltration via package-index and DNS channels and prototype egress allowlisting tied to taint-tracking of sensitive context entering the sandbox.

trust_assumption: An agent deployment treats its system prompt, tool schemas, and guardrail instructions as a confidential control layer the user cannot read, so its injection defenses are designed assuming the attacker is operating blind to the exact wording and tool surface they must defeat.
violation: The attacker first extracts the hidden system prompt and tool definitions (via leakage prompts, refusal-boundary probing, error messages, or token-level reconstruction), then crafts an injection that precisely targets the disclosed instruction wording and tool names, collapsing the security-by-obscurity gap the defenses silently depended on.

techniques.name: System-prompt and tool-schema extraction enabling tailored injection
Refusal-boundary probing to reconstruct hidden guardrail wording

rationale: Most deployed agents implicitly rely on the secrecy of their system prompt and tool inventory for injection resistance, but extraction is reliably achievable and the leaked specifics (exact instruction phrasing, tool names, argument schemas) turn a generic injection into a surgical one that names the very rules and tools it must subvert. The seam is a provenance failure: defenders trust that the control layer is unobservable. Tooling exists to attempt extraction but not to measure how much a given deployment's defense degrades once its prompt is known, and there is no detection that links an earlier extraction-style interaction to a later precisely-targeted injection from the same actor.
suggested_research: Measure injection success rate with vs without prior system-prompt knowledge across agent products to quantify the obscurity dependency; build a detector that correlates extraction-probing sessions with subsequent tailored injection attempts.

trust_assumption: When a parent agent spawns a sub-agent and passes along its task context, it assumes that context is sanitized internal state, so the sub-agent treats inherited instructions and data as trusted parent tasking rather than as content that may have been shaped by upstream untrusted input.
violation: Untrusted content the parent absorbed (a poisoned document, web result, or prior injection) rides into the spawned sub-agent's context unfiltered, and the sub-agent — often holding different or broader tools — executes attacker intent as if it were authentic parent direction, with no boundary re-validation at the spawn.

techniques.name: Unsanitized context inheritance across agent spawn boundary
Privilege/tool-scope escalation via sub-agent with broader capabilities

rationale: Orchestration frameworks (planner/worker, supervisor/sub-agent, fan-out research patterns) pass context across spawn boundaries as if it were trusted internal handoff, but that context routinely contains untrusted material the parent ingested; the spawn is a trust-inheritance point with no re-sanitization, and the sub-agent may have a different tool scope, so injection can effectively escalate by jumping to a more capable agent. This is distinct from inter-agent trust-token forgery (peer authentication) — the failure here is taint propagation through context inheritance within a single trust domain. No framework taint-tracks content across spawns, and detection is absent because the sub-agent's actions look like legitimate delegated work.
suggested_research: Instrument popular multi-agent frameworks to taint-track untrusted content across spawn boundaries; demonstrate a parent->sub-agent escalation where the sub-agent's broader tools execute injected intent, and prototype context-sanitization/scope-narrowing at spawn.

trust_assumption: A RAG or agentic-search system presents citations as verifiable provenance, so the human consumer trusts that a cited source link substantiates the accompanying claim and that the agent quoted or grounded the claim in that real source.
violation: The agent emits a fabricated or mismatched citation — a plausible but nonexistent source, a real URL that does not actually support the claim, or an attacker-planted document crafted to look authoritative — and the human accepts the unverified claim precisely because it appears cited, laundering false or attacker-chosen content through the trust signal of provenance.

techniques.name: Fabricated or mismatched citation laundering an unsupported claim
Attacker-planted authoritative-looking source for citation hijack

rationale: Citations are sold as the answer to LLM hallucination, so users and downstream automations lower their scrutiny on cited claims, yet the citation is generated by the same untrusted-text-conditioned model and is frequently not actually checked against the linked source; this converts the provenance signal into an attack surface where a wrong or attacker-supplied citation makes false content more trusted, not less. The defensive assumption is that 'cited' implies 'grounded.' Tooling that auto-verifies whether a cited source genuinely entails the claim is nascent at best, and there is no detection for the human-facing failure mode where a confidently-cited but unsupported claim drives a decision; SOC and content-integrity tooling do not inspect citation-claim entailment.
suggested_research: Build a citation-entailment verifier that fetches each cited source and scores whether it supports the claim, measure fabrication/mismatch rates across deployed RAG products, and test whether displaying entailment scores changes human over-trust on cited output.
